stem: A previously well 5-year-old girl is brought to a suburban Melbourne general practice with a 4-day history of crusted sores around the nose and right cheek that started after a minor scratch in the school playground. She is systemically well, eating normally, and attending school. She has no drug allergies, no eczema and no household members with skin disease. On examination she is afebrile with a temperature of 36.8 deg C, heart rate 92, respiratory rate 18 and oxygen saturation 99% in room air. There are three discrete lesions less than 3 cm in total area with classic honey-coloured crust, no surrounding cellulitis, no bullae, no lymphangitis and palpable but non-tender submandibular nodes less than 1 cm. There are no other skin lesions and no mucosal involvement. She lives in a non-remote setting with no Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander identification recorded.

data_table.Examination: Three honey-crusted lesions <3 cm total area on face, no cellulitis, no bullae, no mucosal involvement
data_table.Social: Non-remote setting, attends school, no household skin disease

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Topical mupirocin 2% ointment to lesions three times daily for 5 days with cover or removal until lesions are dry
options.rationale: Localised non-bullous impetigo in a non-remote setting with less than three lesion clusters is treated topically with mupirocin or hydrogen peroxide cream per eTG, with exclusion from school until lesions are covered or have crusted (eTG: Impetigo; RCH CPG: Impetigo).

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg up to 500 mg twice daily for 7 days plus daily chlorhexidine washes for the household
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are reserved for extensive disease, systemic features or remote and high-prevalence settings; cefalexin is also broader than required when first-line oral is flucloxacillin (eTG: Impetigo).

options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4/20 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days as community-acquired MRSA cover
options.rationale: Empiric MRSA cover is not indicated for localised non-bullous impetigo in a low-prevalence non-remote setting; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is reserved when MRSA is confirmed or strongly suspected (eTG: Skin and soft tissue infections).

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg up to 2 g six-hourly with admission for paediatric observation
options.rationale: IV antibiotics and admission are reserved for severe cellulitis, abscess, systemic toxicity or failure of oral therapy, none of which apply to this well child with three small crusted lesions (RCH CPG: Cellulitis).

options.text: Reassurance and observation with no antimicrobial therapy and return in 7 days if not resolved
options.rationale: Impetigo is contagious and progresses; treatment shortens duration and limits spread to household and school contacts, so deferring all therapy is inappropriate (RCH CPG: Impetigo).

explanation.summary: Localised non-bullous impetigo in a well child in a non-remote Australian setting is treated topically with mupirocin (or hydrogen peroxide cream) and exclusion from school until lesions are crusted or covered, per eTG and RCH (eTG: Impetigo).
key_points: Topical first-line for limited disease in non-remote settings (eTG: Impetigo).
Oral flucloxacillin (or cefalexin if non-severe penicillin allergy) for extensive, systemic or high-prevalence settings (eTG: Impetigo).
School exclusion until lesions covered or crusted (NHMRC Staying Healthy).
explanation.pearls: Streptococcus pyogenes impetigo carries a small risk of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis - urinalysis any oedema/hypertension after impetigo (RCH CPG: Impetigo).

subtopic_detail: Non-bullous impetigo first-line therapy in primary care

stem: A 10-month-old fully immunised infant is reviewed in general practice. He has had three days of fever to 39.6 deg C without other localising symptoms. He fed reasonably and remained alert between fever spikes. Today the fever has settled and a pink, blanching macular and maculopapular rash has appeared on the trunk, spreading sparsely to the neck and proximal limbs but sparing the face, palms and soles. He has no conjunctivitis, no Koplik spots, no cough or coryza, no lymphadenopathy, and no oral lesions. His mother had no febrile illness in pregnancy. Examination is otherwise unremarkable with normal vitals for age. There is no travel history and no known sick contacts beyond his older sibling at daycare. He has no eczema and no chronic illness.

data_table.Examination: Pink blanching macular rash on trunk, sparing face/palms/soles; no conjunctivitis, no Koplik spots, no oral lesions
data_table.Immunisations: Up to date including MMR not yet due

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Measles with onset of the classic post-coryzal rash now becoming generalised
options.rationale: Measles presents with high fever PLUS cough, coryza, conjunctivitis and Koplik spots, with rash starting on the face and spreading caudally - absent here (NCIRS: Measles).

options.text: Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum) due to human herpesvirus 6
options.rationale: Three days of high fever in a 6-24 month-old followed by defervescence and a truncal blanching maculopapular rash sparing the face is the classic course of HHV-6 roseola (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).

options.text: Rubella with post-auricular lymphadenopathy and rash spreading from the face
options.rationale: Rubella rash starts on the face and spreads caudally and is typically accompanied by tender post-auricular or sub-occipital lymphadenopathy, absent in this infant (NCIRS: Rubella).

options.text: Erythema infectiosum (parvovirus B19) with slapped-cheek facial rash
options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 typically presents in school-age children with a slapped-cheek facial appearance followed by a lacy reticular rash on the limbs, not a truncal rash sparing the face after defervescence (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).

options.text: Scarlet fever from Group A streptococcal pharyngitis with a sandpaper rash
options.rationale: Scarlet fever produces a fine erythematous sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue, perioral pallor and a preceding sore throat, none of which are present (eTG: Scarlet fever).

explanation.summary: The illness arc of high fever for 3 days followed by abrupt defervescence and emergence of a truncal blanching maculopapular rash in a 6-24 month-old is the hallmark of HHV-6 roseola (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
key_points: Fever-then-rash sequence with sparing of the face = roseola (RCH).
No specific treatment; HHV-6 may trigger febrile convulsions (RCH CPG: Febrile convulsion).
Measles, rubella and parvovirus have face-onset or distinct prodromes (NCIRS).
explanation.pearls: Roseola is the commonest exanthem in the first 2 years - if the infant looks well at the time of rash with no fever, you have your answer.

subtopic_detail: Roseola infantum versus alternative febrile exanthems in infancy

stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to a GP clinic with one day of low-grade fever, reduced oral intake and new tender oral and skin lesions. Her older brother attended childcare with a known outbreak of hand foot and mouth disease last week. She is fully immunised and previously well, with no eczema. On examination she has a temperature of 38.1 deg C and is comfortable. There are several shallow oral ulcers on the soft palate and buccal mucosa and small grey-roofed vesicles surrounded by an erythematous halo on the palms, soles and around the buttocks. There is no rash on the trunk, face or scalp, no lymphadenopathy, no conjunctivitis and no genital or perianal blistering. Heart rate is 104, respiratory rate 22, oxygen saturation 99% in room air and capillary refill is brisk. Her mother is currently 30 weeks pregnant and asks specifically about risk and about when her daughter can return to childcare.

data_table.Examination: Shallow oral ulcers; grey-roofed vesicles on palms, soles, buttocks; no truncal rash

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combined diagnosis and exclusion advice?

options.text: Hand foot and mouth disease; exclude from childcare until all blisters have dried
options.rationale: The clinical picture and exposure history are classical for enteroviral HFMD and NHMRC Staying Healthy advises exclusion until all blisters have dried (NHMRC Staying Healthy; RCH CPG).

options.text: Primary herpes simplex gingivostomatitis; exclude until afebrile and lesions crusted
options.rationale: Primary HSV gingivostomatitis produces clustered anterior oral vesicles, gingival inflammation and bleeding without acral palm/sole lesions, which makes HSV unlikely here (RCH CPG: Cold sores and gingivostomatitis).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cold sores and gingivostomatitis

options.text: Varicella (chickenpox); exclude for at least 5 days from rash onset and until lesions crusted
options.rationale: Varicella produces a generalised crops of vesicles on the trunk, face and scalp evolving through stages, not an acral vesicular eruption sparing the trunk (NCIRS: Varicella).

options.text: Erythema multiforme minor; no exclusion required as the condition is non-infectious
options.rationale: Erythema multiforme has target lesions on the limbs without oral vesicles in this distribution and the childcare exposure makes HFMD far more likely (DermNet NZ: Erythema multiforme).

options.text: Streptococcal pharyngitis with reactive rash; exclude for 24 hours after antibiotic initiation
options.rationale: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis presents with exudative tonsillitis and tender cervical lymphadenopathy without acral vesicles, so this clinical picture is wrong (eTG: Pharyngitis).

explanation.summary: Acute febrile illness with vesicular lesions on palms, soles, buttocks plus oral enanthem in a child exposed to a childcare outbreak is HFMD; NHMRC advises exclusion from childcare until all blisters have dried (NHMRC Staying Healthy).
key_points: Coxsackie A16 and enterovirus 71 most commonly responsible (RCH CPG).
Supportive care - oral analgesia, hydration; avoid mucosal lignocaine in young children (RCH CPG: Cold sores and gingivostomatitis).
Pregnancy contact: HFMD is not associated with congenital malformations; reassure (RANZCOG patient information).
explanation.pearls: Eczema coxsackium - widespread vesicular eruption in atopic skin - mimics eczema herpeticum but is enteroviral.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Cold sores and gingivostomatitis

subtopic_detail: Hand foot and mouth disease recognition and exclusion advice

stem: A 6-year-old girl is seen with a 24-hour history of bilateral facial rash following a mild flu-like illness last week. She has had no fever, is eating and drinking normally, and is attending school as usual. On examination she has a confluent red rash on both cheeks with circumoral sparing and a faint lacy, reticular rash on the extensor surfaces of both forearms. She is afebrile with normal vital signs for age and otherwise systemically well. Her mother is 22 weeks pregnant and her younger brother has known sickle cell disease and a baseline haemoglobin of 78 g/L. The school has reported recent cases of slapped cheek disease. She has no eczema, no joint swelling and no pallor. There is no recent overseas travel.

data_table.Examination: Confluent erythematous rash both cheeks with circumoral sparing; lacy reticular rash on extensor forearms
data_table.Household: Mother 22/40 pregnant; brother has sickle cell disease (Hb baseline 78 g/L)

lead_in: Which household contact warrants the most urgent specific action?

options.text: Reassure all family members - the patient is no longer infectious once the rash appears
options.rationale: Although the index case is no longer infectious once the rash is established, the question is about household risk; reassurance alone does not address the pregnant mother and the brother with sickle cell disease who were exposed during the infectious viraemic phase last week (RCH CPG: Parvovirus B19).

options.text: Brother with sickle cell disease - monitor and arrange urgent FBC for transient aplastic crisis
options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 arrests erythroid precursors and precipitates transient aplastic crisis in patients with chronic haemolysis such as sickle cell disease; an FBC and reticulocyte count are warranted urgently if symptomatic anaemia develops (RCH CPG: Parvovirus B19; eTG: Parvovirus).

options.text: Mother - immediate IVIG to prevent foetal hydrops irrespective of her immunity status
options.rationale: First-line management of maternal parvovirus exposure is serology to determine susceptibility; if non-immune and seroconverting, serial foetal ultrasound for middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity is used to detect anaemia - not empiric IVIG (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Mother - urgent termination counselling because of high foetal loss risk in the second trimester
options.rationale: Foetal loss risk from maternal parvovirus is highest in the first 20 weeks but remains low overall (<10%); management is serological testing then surveillance, not termination counselling (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).

options.text: Index patient - exclude from school for 5 further days because she is now most infectious
options.rationale: By the time the slapped-cheek rash appears, viraemia has resolved and the child is no longer infectious - no exclusion is required (NHMRC Staying Healthy).

explanation.summary: Erythema infectiosum is parvovirus B19 - by the time the slapped-cheek rash is visible, the index case is non-infectious. Highest-priority household action is to monitor the sibling with sickle cell disease for transient aplastic crisis (RCH CPG: Parvovirus B19).
key_points: Index no longer infectious once rash appears (NHMRC).
Aplastic crisis in chronic haemolytic anaemia (sickle cell, hereditary spherocytosis) - check FBC + reticulocyte (eTG).
Maternal exposure: serology first; if seroconversion, foetal MCA Doppler surveillance for hydrops (RANZCOG).
explanation.pearls: Adults often manifest as symmetrical polyarthritis rather than slapped cheek - consider parvovirus in PJM (post-viral arthritis) workup.

subtopic_detail: Erythema infectiosum and household risk stratification

stem: A 4-year-old boy is reviewed in a community paediatric allergy clinic after two episodes of hives, lip swelling and one episode of vomiting and audible wheeze within 20 minutes of eating egg. There has been no hypotension or collapse and both reactions resolved with oral cetirizine and one episode required an emergency department adrenaline injection. Skin prick test today shows a wheal of 8 mm to egg white and specific IgE to egg white is 22 kU/L. He has well-controlled mild persistent asthma and takes a low-dose ICS daily. His weight is 17 kg and he has no other allergies. His childcare requires written medical instructions. The family has not previously been prescribed an adrenaline autoinjector. Both parents have completed an asthma first aid course.

data_table.Investigations: SPT egg white wheal 8 mm; sIgE egg white 22 kU/L
data_table.History: Two reactions to egg with wheeze and one ED adrenaline; mild persistent asthma on low-dose ICS

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ASCIA documentation and device prescription?

options.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions (no adrenaline autoinjector) plus oral cetirizine as needed
options.rationale: This plan is for confirmed allergy without prescribed adrenaline; documented anaphylaxis (wheeze) and asthma comorbidity mandate progression to an autoinjector and an Anaphylaxis Action Plan (ASCIA Action Plans).

options.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis with an EpiPen Jr (150 microgram) adrenaline autoinjector
options.rationale: Documented anaphylaxis with respiratory involvement plus comorbid asthma is an indication for an adrenaline autoinjector; ASCIA recommends the 150 microgram device for children weighing 7.5 to 20 kg and the Anaphylaxis Action Plan for any patient prescribed an autoinjector (ASCIA Action Plans; ASCIA Adrenaline Autoinjector Prescribing Guidelines).

options.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis with an EpiPen 300 microgram adrenaline autoinjector
options.rationale: EpiPen 300 microgram is appropriate from 20 kg upwards under ASCIA guidance; at 17 kg the 150 microgram device is recommended (ASCIA Adrenaline Autoinjector Prescribing Guidelines).

options.text: Refer for oral food challenge to egg with no action plan until the result is known
options.rationale: Recent objective reaction with wheeze plus large skin prick test and elevated specific IgE makes a confirmatory challenge unsafe and unnecessary; the priority is to provide an action plan and autoinjector (ASCIA Food Allergy Position Statements).

options.text: Discharge with an ASCIA Asthma First Aid Plan only as wheeze is presumed asthma-driven
options.rationale: Wheeze occurring within 20 minutes of a known food trigger is anaphylaxis until proven otherwise; an Asthma First Aid Plan does not address the food trigger or adrenaline (ASCIA Action Plans).

explanation.summary: Confirmed IgE-mediated egg allergy with respiratory involvement (anaphylaxis) plus asthma is a clear indication for an adrenaline autoinjector and an ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan; device strength is weight-based and 150 microgram covers 7.5-20 kg (ASCIA Adrenaline Autoinjector Prescribing Guidelines).
key_points: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis (red) for any patient prescribed an autoinjector (ASCIA).
EpiPen Jr 150 microgram for 7.5-20 kg; EpiPen 300 microgram for >20 kg (ASCIA).
Asthma comorbidity increases anaphylaxis severity and lowers threshold for autoinjector prescription (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: Two devices are funded under the PBS for adrenaline autoinjector prescription in Australia.

subtopic_detail: ASCIA Action Plan selection in IgE-mediated food allergy

stem: A 2-year-old boy with established atopic dermatitis returns to the GP for review after 6 weeks on hydrocortisone 1% applied sparingly once daily to active flares and intermittent moisturiser use. His mother reports nightly scratching, broken sleep for the whole family, and frequent flares despite avoiding soap and using cotton clothing. There is no honey-coloured crust, no clustered vesicles, no fever and no surrounding cellulitic spread. He is otherwise well, fully immunised, has no food allergies and has a normal growth trajectory. Examination shows extensive erythema, lichenification and excoriation in the antecubital and popliteal fossae and on the cheeks, covering approximately 25 percent of the body surface area, with no overt secondary infection and no peri-orbital involvement. The mother is hesitant about stronger topical steroids because of online concerns about skin thinning, growth suppression and steroid-induced adrenal failure. She has been using a single 50 g tube of bland moisturiser per week, applied only when the skin looks visibly dry. She has not been shown the fingertip-unit technique.

data_table.Examination: Erythema, lichenification, excoriation in flexures and cheeks; BSA ~25%; no honey crust, no vesicles
data_table.Current therapy: Hydrocortisone 1% sparingly once daily; moisturiser intermittently (50 g/week)

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue hydrocortisone 1% ointment once daily to active eczema and add sedating oral cetirizine 5 mg nightly for pruritus with written instruction to limit moisturiser to dry areas
options.rationale: Sedating antihistamines are not recommended for itch in paediatric eczema and continuing a clearly insufficient potency in moderate eczema perpetuates flares (RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA: Eczema).

options.text: Step up to methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment daily to active eczema with at least 250 g per week of bland moisturiser and written fingertip-unit instructions
options.rationale: Moderate eczema unresponsive to a weak corticosteroid warrants stepping up to a moderately potent agent such as methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% applied daily to active areas, with generous emollient use (>=250 g/week for a young child) and structured fingertip-unit education to address steroid phobia (RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA: Eczema; eTG: Atopic dermatitis).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 7 days to break the flare cycle followed by tapering wean over a further week, continuing hydrocortisone 1% once daily
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids cause rebound flares on withdrawal and are not recommended in paediatric atopic dermatitis except in exceptional severe acute disease under specialist care (ASCIA: Eczema; eTG: Atopic dermatitis).

options.text: Start topical tacrolimus 0.03% ointment twice daily to all active areas and stop topical corticosteroids entirely while continuing intermittent emollient as the sole adjunct
options.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are useful steroid-sparing agents in flexures and on the face but are second line in Australian practice and not normally used as monotherapy to replace corticosteroids in moderate disease (eTG: Atopic dermatitis; ASCIA).

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric dermatology this week for systemic immunomodulation with methotrexate or ciclosporin without further trial of stepped-up topical corticosteroid therapy
options.rationale: Systemic immunomodulators are reserved for severe disease that has failed optimised topical therapy and phototherapy under paediatric dermatology; appropriate topical escalation has not yet been trialled (RCH CPG: Eczema).

explanation.summary: Moderate paediatric eczema failing weak topical corticosteroids requires stepping up to a moderately potent ointment, generous emollient use (~250 g/week in a young child) and explicit fingertip-unit instruction to overcome steroid phobia (RCH CPG: Eczema).
key_points: Potency, not duration, is the primary lever when low-potency fails (RCH).
Emollient quantity is the single biggest predictor of flare frequency (RCH; ASCIA).
One fingertip unit covers approximately two adult palm areas (RCH CPG: Eczema).
explanation.pearls: Methylprednisolone aceponate has a lower-systemic-absorption profile making it a workhorse for moderate paediatric eczema.

subtopic_detail: Eczema severity step-up using fingertip units and topical potency

stem: A first-time mother brings her 5-month-old breastfed infant to the GP for advice. The infant has moderate atopic dermatitis well-controlled on regular emollient and intermittent methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% under paediatric dermatology supervision. There is no history of immediate reactions to any food and no breast-milk-related symptoms. The mother and father both have hay fever and the mother has mild asthma. The paternal grandfather has confirmed peanut allergy. The infant is starting to show developmental signs of readiness for solids - good head control, interest in food, loss of tongue thrust. She has read conflicting online advice about delaying highly allergenic foods, especially peanut, until age 1 to 'wait until the gut is more mature'. She asks specifically how and when peanut should be introduced. She is not vegan and there is no cultural restriction. She specifically asks whether peanut should be deferred until 12 months because of the family history, and whether referral for skin prick testing should occur before any home introduction. The family wants a clear, written, ASCIA-aligned plan to give the childcare centre.

data_table.Background: 5-month-old, moderate eczema controlled on emollient + moderate TCS
data_table.Family history: Both parents atopic; paternal grandfather peanut allergic

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding allergenic solid introduction?

options.text: Introduce smooth peanut butter or peanut paste from around 6 months while continuing breastfeeding and continue including it regularly in the diet
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends introducing common allergens including peanut, egg, dairy and tree nuts in the first year (around 6 months but not before 4 months) regardless of family history, and ongoing regular inclusion sustains tolerance (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
source_refs: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention

options.text: Delay peanut introduction until 12 months of age given the strong family history of peanut allergy and continue exclusive breastfeeding with avoidance of all tree nuts
options.rationale: Delaying allergen introduction increases not decreases food allergy risk; ASCIA explicitly advises against this approach (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
source_refs: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention

options.text: Refer for skin prick testing to peanut and other common allergens before any home introduction is attempted, and defer introduction pending the result
options.rationale: Pre-screening with skin prick testing in asymptomatic infants is not recommended because it identifies sensitisation, not clinical allergy, and may lead to unnecessary avoidance (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
source_refs: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention

options.text: Introduce peanut only in the supervised setting of the GP clinic given moderate eczema and parental atopy, with observation for 2 hours after the first dose
options.rationale: Routine in-clinic supervised introduction is not recommended for asymptomatic infants and is impractical; home introduction following ASCIA advice is appropriate (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
source_refs: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention

options.text: Replace breastfeeding with extensively hydrolysed formula from now to prevent food allergy and delay solid introduction until at least 9 months of age
options.rationale: Hydrolysed formulas are not recommended for prevention of allergy and there is no indication to interrupt successful breastfeeding (NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines; ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention

explanation.summary: Introducing common allergenic foods including peanut in the first year of life (around 6 months, never before 4 months) and continuing regular exposure is the evidence-based primary prevention strategy endorsed by ASCIA and NHMRC (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).
key_points: Early introduction reduces peanut allergy risk (LEAP-aligned ASCIA position).
Continued regular intake is required to maintain tolerance (ASCIA).
Eczema severity increases risk of food allergy but is NOT a contraindication to early introduction (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: Whole nuts are a choking hazard under age 5 - use smooth peanut butter or paste.

sources.label: ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention

subtopic_detail: Atopic march counselling and early peanut introduction

stem: A 3-year-old girl with previously confirmed IgE-mediated cow's milk allergy attends a paediatric allergy day-stay for a supervised oral food challenge. Her most recent reaction was 15 months ago with urticaria but no respiratory symptoms. She has well-controlled mild eczema and no asthma. Skin prick test today shows a wheal of 4 mm to cow's milk extract (negative control 0 mm, histamine 6 mm). Specific IgE to cow's milk is 1.2 kU/L. She remains on a milk-free diet with calcium-fortified soy. After incremental doses up to a cumulative 200 mL of full-fat cow's milk over 3 hours she develops two small urticarial wheals on the trunk, no peri-oral swelling, no wheeze, no vomiting, no abdominal pain, and remains haemodynamically stable. Repeat observation at 90 minutes shows no progression and the wheals resolve with oral cetirizine. The family asks whether milk can now be reintroduced freely at home or whether further specialist input is required before any dietary change. They request a written plan for childcare and for grandparents.

data_table.Pre-challenge: SPT cow's milk 4 mm; sIgE 1.2 kU/L; no asthma; last reaction 15 months ago (urticaria only)
data_table.Challenge response: Two urticarial wheals at 200 mL; no respiratory, GI, mucosal or cardiovascular features; resolved with cetirizine

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate interpretation and ongoing plan?

options.text: Failed challenge - continue strict milk avoidance and re-challenge in 12-18 months with an ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis and EpiPen Jr
options.rationale: Any objective allergic reaction during a supervised oral food challenge constitutes a failed challenge; the child remains allergic, milk avoidance continues, and an action plan with autoinjector is required pending repeat assessment in 12-18 months (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges; ASCIA Action Plans).

options.text: Passed challenge - introduce cow's milk fully into the diet from tomorrow with no further action and discontinue calcium-fortified soy entirely without ongoing specialist follow-up
options.rationale: Reproducible urticaria during the challenge meets criteria for a failed challenge; this child is still allergic and unrestricted dietary milk would be unsafe (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges).

options.text: Equivocal challenge - commence the baked milk ladder at home over the next 6 weeks without further specialist input and reintroduce fresh milk if no symptoms arise
options.rationale: A baked milk ladder is a specialist-directed protocol and is not the appropriate immediate response to a failed open challenge; isolated home titration without supervision is unsafe (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges; ASCIA: Milk allergy).

options.text: Passed challenge - replace soy with cow's milk over 7 days, reduce calcium supplementation and discharge from allergy follow-up with no autoinjector required
options.rationale: This is incorrect because the challenge failed and substitution of cow's milk would expose the child to ongoing risk of reactions (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges).

options.text: Indeterminate challenge - administer same-day intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg and admit for 24 hours of cardiorespiratory observation before any further dietary advice
options.rationale: IM adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis; isolated cutaneous reactions without respiratory, cardiovascular or persistent GI features are treated with antihistamine and observation (ASCIA Action Plans; ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7).

explanation.summary: An objective allergic reaction (even isolated urticaria) during a supervised challenge means the challenge has failed; ongoing avoidance with an Anaphylaxis Action Plan and adrenaline autoinjector is appropriate, with re-evaluation in 12-18 months (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges).
key_points: Failed = ANY reproducible objective reaction (ASCIA).
Cutaneous-only reaction is not anaphylaxis but still constitutes failed challenge (ASCIA; ANZCOR).
Repeat challenge timing depends on age, severity and trajectory of sIgE/SPT (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: Baked milk and baked egg ladders rely on conformational epitope denaturation by heat - around 70% of milk and egg-allergic children tolerate baked forms.

subtopic_detail: Oral food challenge interpretation for resolving cow's milk allergy

stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by his parents 2 hours after the sudden onset of widespread itchy wheals on the trunk, limbs and face, plus puffiness of the lips and right eyelid. He took ibuprofen 3 hours ago for a viral upper respiratory tract infection that started 4 days ago. He has no food triggers, no insect exposure and no recent new medications other than ibuprofen and a 2-day course of amoxicillin started yesterday for presumed otitis media. He has no asthma. On arrival his temperature is 37.4 deg C, heart rate 100, respiratory rate 22, oxygen saturation 99% in room air and BP 105/68. He is settled, talking in sentences, with no stridor, no wheeze, no drooling, no tongue swelling and a normal voice. Lung auscultation is clear and he has brisk capillary refill. There is no abdominal pain, no vomiting and no hypotension.

data_table.Examination: Widespread wheals, lip and right eyelid swelling; no stridor, no wheeze, no tongue swelling, normal voice
data_table.Exposures: Ibuprofen 3 hours ago; amoxicillin day 2 for OM; viral URTI day 4

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 1:1000 0.3 mg into the mid anterolateral thigh, IV fluid bolus 20 mL/kg normal saline and admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring overnight
options.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis (respiratory, cardiovascular or persistent severe GI involvement); this patient has urticaria with angioedema but no airway compromise or hypotension (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine), withhold the ibuprofen and amoxicillin, observe for 4 hours and discharge with action plan if stable
options.rationale: Acute urticaria with angioedema but without anaphylaxis features is managed with a non-sedating H1 antihistamine, removal of suspected triggers (NSAID and likely amoxicillin in the setting of a viral URTI which can transiently amplify drug-rash risk), and observation; an ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions is appropriate at discharge (eTG: Urticaria; ASCIA: Urticaria; RCH CPG: Urticaria).

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat plus oral promethazine 0.25 mg/kg as a sedating antihistamine, with discharge if asymptomatic at 2 hours after a single dose
options.rationale: Sedating antihistamines such as promethazine are not recommended in children under 2 and are not first line in acute urticaria; corticosteroids have no acute benefit in uncomplicated urticaria (ASCIA: Urticaria; RCH CPG: Urticaria).

options.text: Continue both amoxicillin and ibuprofen at current doses, add an oral non-sedating antihistamine for symptom control, and counsel that viral exanthem is the most likely cause
options.rationale: Continuing the NSAID and antibiotic that temporally preceded the urticaria is unsafe pending allergy assessment; the suspected triggers should be withheld (ASCIA: Drug allergy in children).

options.text: Refer for outpatient intramuscular omalizumab as first-line acute therapy for urticaria with angioedema and discharge after a single dose of oral antihistamine
options.rationale: Omalizumab is a specialist treatment for chronic spontaneous urticaria failing high-dose antihistamines, not an acute ED therapy (ASCIA: Urticaria).

explanation.summary: Acute urticaria with angioedema and no airway, respiratory or cardiovascular compromise is treated with a non-sedating antihistamine and trigger removal; adrenaline is reserved for true anaphylaxis (eTG: Urticaria).
key_points: Distinguish urticaria/angioedema from anaphylaxis by absence of respiratory/CV features (ASCIA).
Withhold suspected NSAID and antibiotic triggers; document for outpatient drug allergy assessment (ASCIA).
Steroids and sedating antihistamines are not first-line in acute paediatric urticaria (RCH; ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: Up to 80% of paediatric acute urticaria is viral, but co-exposure to NSAIDs or antibiotics complicates causality - delayed structured testing is needed.

subtopic_detail: Acute urticaria with angioedema in the ED

stem: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in general practice with a 6-week history of itchy, sharply demarcated dermatitis confined to a thin horizontal band across the upper abdomen and to both earlobes. The rash started 3 weeks after she received costume jewellery and a new metallic belt buckle for her birthday. There is no involvement of the rest of the trunk, no involvement of the hands or wrists, no joint pain, no oral lesions, no mucosal involvement, no systemic upset and no atopic personal or family history. She is otherwise well, fully immunised, on no medications and has no history of eczema. Examination shows pink, scaly, lichenified plaques in the exact distribution of the buckle and earring posts with crisp angular borders and no spread to surrounding skin. Hand washing is performed with standard soap several times a day with no rash. Application of bland moisturiser has not helped.

data_table.Examination: Sharply demarcated, scaly, lichenified plaques confined to belt-buckle line on abdomen and to both earlobes; no spread, no mucosal involvement
data_table.History: New costume jewellery and metallic belt buckle 3 weeks before onset
data_table.Other: No atopy, no eczema, no systemic symptoms

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate confirmatory investigation?

options.text: Allergic contact dermatitis to nickel with confirmatory referral for standard-series patch testing
options.rationale: Sharply demarcated dermatitis confined to sites of contact with costume jewellery and a belt buckle is highly suggestive of nickel allergic contact dermatitis, the most common contact allergen in Australian children; gold-standard confirmation is patch testing (Australasian Contact Dermatitis Society; DermNet NZ: Allergic contact dermatitis).

options.text: Irritant contact dermatitis from soap with skin scraping for fungal microscopy
options.rationale: Irritant dermatitis from soap typically affects the hands and wrists, not the belt line and earlobes; fungal scraping would be relevant for tinea, which is annular with central clearing (DermNet NZ: Irritant contact dermatitis).

options.text: Atopic dermatitis in flexural distribution with referral for serum total IgE
options.rationale: Atopic dermatitis preferentially involves antecubital and popliteal flexures, not contact-defined geometric patches; serum total IgE is not diagnostic of any condition in isolation (RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA).

options.text: Tinea corporis with Wood's lamp examination at the bedside today
options.rationale: Tinea is typically annular with raised scaly borders and central clearing, and most dermatophytes do not fluoresce under Wood's lamp; the distribution is also wrong (eTG: Dermatophyte infections; DermNet NZ).

options.text: Discoid nummular eczema with direct immunofluorescence biopsy for confirmation
options.rationale: Discoid eczema presents with multiple coin-shaped lesions in random distribution; direct immunofluorescence is for immunobullous disease, not contact dermatitis (DermNet NZ: Discoid eczema).

explanation.summary: Geometrically demarcated dermatitis matching contact-object footprint plus a temporal link to nickel-containing items strongly suggests nickel allergic contact dermatitis; patch testing is the gold-standard confirmatory test (Australasian Contact Dermatitis Society).
key_points: Allergic contact = delayed (type IV) hypersensitivity confined to contact site (DermNet NZ).
Nickel is the commonest contact allergen in children worldwide (ACDS).
Patch testing reads at 48 and 96 hours; SPT and serum IgE do not diagnose contact allergy (ACDS).
explanation.pearls: Australian standards mandate nickel release limits in jewellery similar to the EU directive - earring posts remain a common culprit.

subtopic_detail: Allergic versus irritant contact dermatitis discrimination in a child

stem: A 4-year-old Aboriginal boy is referred from a remote community clinic to a regional paediatric service with a 6-week history of widespread itch, worse at night, and skin changes. He lives in a household with seven other people, three of whom also have itch. He has had two courses of topical hydrocortisone with no improvement. On examination he is well-grown, afebrile and systemically well. There is hyperkeratotic, fissured scale on the palms, soles and elbows, multiple excoriated papules and a few burrows in finger web spaces and on the wrists. There is no facial or scalp involvement. He has no immune deficiency, no significant past medical history and is fully immunised. Skin scraping under microscopy shows mites and eggs. There is no secondary impetigo. The community has been advised by environmental health that there has been a recent cluster of scabies cases.

data_table.Examination: Hyperkeratotic palms/soles/elbows; burrows in finger webs and wrists; widespread excoriations; no facial or scalp involvement
data_table.Household: 8 people, 3 with itch; remote community with current scabies cluster
data_table.Microscopy: Mites and eggs visualised on scraping

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Permethrin 5% cream applied to the index patient only once now and repeated at 7 days, with continued hydrocortisone 1% for residual itch, no contact treatment and no environmental laundry measures
options.rationale: Failure to treat all household and close contacts simultaneously is the commonest cause of treatment failure; topical steroids do not address the underlying infestation (CARPA Standard Treatment Manual; RCH CPG: Scabies).

options.text: Permethrin 5% cream to the entire body of the patient and all household and close contacts simultaneously, repeat at 7-14 days, plus environmental measures (hot wash of bedding/clothing) and consider oral ivermectin given crusted features
options.rationale: Hyperkeratotic palms/soles in an endemic setting raises suspicion of crusted scabies; Australian guidance (eTG, RCH and CARPA) recommends whole-body permethrin to index plus all contacts simultaneously, repeat dose at 7-14 days, environmental decontamination, and consideration of oral ivermectin in crusted disease (eTG: Scabies; RCH CPG: Scabies; CARPA).

options.text: Oral ivermectin 200 microgram/kg as monotherapy on day 0 and day 7 with no topical scabicide for the index patient or contacts, no environmental decontamination and no school exclusion
options.rationale: Ivermectin is an adjunct, particularly for crusted disease and outbreaks, and does not negate the need for topical scabicide, contact treatment and environmental measures (eTG: Scabies).

options.text: Benzyl benzoate 25% emulsion applied head to toe to the patient and all family members on 7 consecutive nights without repeat dosing, environmental laundry measures or specialist follow-up
options.rationale: Benzyl benzoate is an alternative scabicide but is irritant to paediatric skin and not first line; daily application for a week is also not standard (eTG: Scabies).

options.text: Defer all treatment pending dermatology incisional biopsy for definitive histological confirmation despite the positive scraping result, with no interim scabicide therapy or household contact tracing today
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already confirmed by microscopy showing mites and eggs; biopsy is unnecessary and delays definitive management of an outbreak (RCH CPG: Scabies).

explanation.summary: Crusted scabies (especially in remote Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities) requires simultaneous treatment of the patient and all household and close contacts with topical scabicide plus environmental measures, with oral ivermectin considered for crusted disease and outbreaks (eTG: Scabies; CARPA).
key_points: Treat all contacts simultaneously - lone treatment leads to recurrence (RCH).
Repeat permethrin at 7-14 days to cover newly hatched mites (eTG).
Crusted scabies is highly transmissible and a notifiable cluster trigger in some jurisdictions (CDNA).
explanation.pearls: Itch may persist 2-4 weeks after successful eradication - emollient and short-course low-potency steroid, NOT repeat scabicide.

subtopic_detail: Crusted scabies and household management in a paediatric setting

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents to a general practice with a 4-week history of a progressively enlarging, tender, boggy scalp lesion with overlying pustules and patchy hair loss in the affected area. His older sister recently completed treatment for scalp ringworm at the same school. He has no fever, no headache and no neck stiffness. On examination he has a 4 cm boggy inflammatory plaque on the vertex with several superficial pustules, surrounding scale, broken hairs and tender posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. There is no fluctuant abscess, no facial swelling and no cellulitic spread. Skin scraping and hair pluck for fungal microscopy and culture have been sent. He has no other dermatological disease and no allergies. His mother is worried about scarring alopecia and has read online about needing oral antibiotics for the pustules. The mother specifically asks whether incision and drainage of the boggy lesion is required and whether oral antibiotics for the pustules should be commenced today.

data_table.Examination: 4 cm boggy inflammatory scalp plaque with pustules and broken hairs; tender posterior cervical lymphadenopathy; no fluctuant abscess
data_table.Contacts: Sister recently treated for scalp ringworm

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial treatment plan?

options.text: Oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for at least 4-8 weeks plus a topical antifungal shampoo, with topical/oral antibiotics only if bacterial superinfection is proven
options.rationale: Tinea capitis with kerion always requires systemic antifungal therapy because topical agents do not penetrate the hair follicle; griseofulvin and terbinafine are first-line in Australian guidance and adjunctive antifungal shampoo reduces shedding (eTG: Dermatophyte infections; RCH CPG: Tinea).

options.text: Topical ketoconazole 2% shampoo twice weekly and topical terbinafine 1% cream applied to the scalp lesion daily for 4 weeks with no systemic antifungal therapy
options.rationale: Topical therapy alone is inadequate for tinea capitis because dermatophytes invade hair shafts beyond topical penetration; systemic treatment is mandatory (eTG: Dermatophyte infections).

options.text: Incision and drainage of the boggy kerion under procedural sedation followed by oral flucloxacillin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for 7 days with no antifungal cover
options.rationale: A kerion is an inflammatory dermatophyte reaction, not a bacterial abscess; incision and drainage worsens scarring and antibiotics do not treat the underlying fungal infection (DermNet NZ: Kerion).

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 7 days as monotherapy to reduce scalp inflammation, with no antifungal therapy commenced until fungal culture returns
options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy without antifungal cover is inappropriate and risks worsening the infection; some specialists use steroids ADJUNCTIVELY with antifungal cover for severe kerion (eTG: Dermatophyte infections).

options.text: Watchful waiting for 4 weeks pending the fungal culture result with no antifungal therapy in the interim and review only if the lesion enlarges
options.rationale: Delaying treatment risks permanent scarring alopecia and ongoing community transmission; empirical therapy based on clinical features and microscopy is standard while awaiting culture (RCH CPG: Tinea).

explanation.summary: Tinea capitis with kerion requires SYSTEMIC antifungal therapy (griseofulvin or terbinafine) for several weeks plus adjunctive antifungal shampoo to reduce spore shedding; antibiotics are only added when bacterial superinfection is documented (eTG: Dermatophyte infections).
key_points: Topical therapy alone fails because of hair-shaft involvement (eTG).
Treat household and close contacts with antifungal shampoo to reduce transmission (RCH).
School exclusion is not required once treatment is commenced (NHMRC Staying Healthy).
explanation.pearls: Mistaking a kerion for a bacterial abscess and incising it is a classic exam trap - leave the scalp alone and treat the fungus.

subtopic_detail: Tinea capitis with kerion and systemic antifungal selection

stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to general practice with a 6-week history of a slowly progressive linear band of small, slightly hypopigmented, mildly itchy papules along the side of her right leg. The band starts on the lateral thigh, follows a curving line down to the lateral ankle, does not cross the midline, and has no associated swelling, ulceration or pustules. She is otherwise well, fully immunised, with no relevant past history. There has been no trauma, no insect bites at the site, no new clothing or detergent and no contact with anyone with similar rash. She has no eczema, no asthma and no recognised allergies. Examination shows a 30 cm linear band of pinhead-sized flat-topped papules in the line described, with no scaling, no induration, no nail involvement and no associated lymphadenopathy. The mother is anxious about scarring and is keen to start any available treatment immediately.

data_table.Examination: 30 cm linear band of small flat papules along right leg following lines of Blaschko, no crossing of midline; no scaling, no induration, no nail change
data_table.Symptoms: Mild pruritus only; no systemic features
data_table.History: No trauma, no contact triggers, no atopic history

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Reassurance with bland emollient and a short course of mild topical corticosteroid (e.g. hydrocortisone 1%) for pruritus, with expected spontaneous resolution over 1-2 years and warning that post-inflammatory pigment change may persist
options.rationale: Lichen striatus is a benign self-limiting eruption along lines of Blaschko that typically resolves spontaneously over 6-24 months; mild topical corticosteroid is used only for symptomatic pruritus and the family should be warned that pigmentary change may persist after resolution (DermNet NZ: Lichen striatus; Paediatric Dermatology Update RCH).

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric dermatology for incisional skin biopsy of the band to histologically exclude linear morphoea and other sclerosing dermatoses despite the soft non-indurated examination findings
options.rationale: Linear morphoea is sclerotic, indurated and can cause atrophy or limb-length discrepancy; the soft non-indurated flat-topped papules and mild itch here are classic for lichen striatus and biopsy is rarely required (DermNet NZ: Linear morphoea).

options.text: Commence methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment twice daily for 6 weeks along the entire band with monthly review for tapering, assessment of skin atrophy and consideration of switching to tacrolimus
options.rationale: Moderately potent corticosteroids carry skin-atrophy risk on the limbs of young children and are unnecessary for a benign self-limiting condition (eTG: Atopic dermatitis - corticosteroid principles).

options.text: Refer for systemic immunosuppression with methotrexate given the linear distribution and 6-week chronicity, with weekly folic acid and monthly bloods for liver and renal function monitoring
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression has no role in lichen striatus, which is self-limiting and benign (DermNet NZ: Lichen striatus).

options.text: Treat with topical clotrimazole 1% antifungal cream applied to the band twice daily for 4 weeks as for cutaneous candidiasis with review at 2 weeks and oral fluconazole if no response
options.rationale: Candidiasis is intertriginous, moist and not arranged along lines of Blaschko; the morphology does not fit (eTG: Candidiasis).

explanation.summary: Lichen striatus is a benign, self-limiting eruption of small flat papules arranged along the lines of Blaschko in pre-school and primary-school children; the management is reassurance plus mild symptomatic topical care (DermNet NZ: Lichen striatus).
key_points: Distribution along lines of Blaschko is the diagnostic feature (DermNet NZ).
Resolution usually within 6-24 months; pigmentary change can persist (DermNet NZ).
Differentiate from linear morphoea (sclerotic, indurated) and inflammatory linear verrucous epidermal naevus (persistent, verrucous).
explanation.pearls: Lines of Blaschko reflect embryonic ectodermal migration patterns - they do NOT correspond to dermatomes.

subtopic_detail: Lichen striatus recognition and management

stem: A 14-year-old girl recently arrived from a country without routine MMR vaccination presents to a community health clinic with a 2-day history of fine pink maculopapular rash that began on the face and is spreading caudally. She has had a mild fever, a sore throat, and tender lumps behind both ears for 24 hours preceding the rash, along with mild joint discomfort in the hands. She has not had any specific food trigger and there are no medications. She is otherwise well, eating, drinking and ambulant with stable vital signs (T 37.8, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 99%). On examination she has discrete tender post-auricular and sub-occipital lymphadenopathy, mild conjunctival injection, no Koplik spots, mild oropharyngeal redness, and a fading erythematous fine rash now on the trunk. There are no purpura, no oral ulcers and no joint swelling. The clinic has several pregnant women attending today.

data_table.Examination: Fine maculopapular rash spreading from face to trunk; tender post-auricular and sub-occipital lymphadenopathy; mild conjunctival injection; no Koplik spots, no purpura
data_table.History: Unvaccinated; recent arrival from country without routine MMR; pregnant women in waiting room

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate public-health action?

options.text: Isolate the patient immediately, notify the local public health unit on clinical suspicion, and identify and counsel any pregnant contacts about rubella serology
options.rationale: Rubella is a notifiable disease in all Australian jurisdictions on clinical suspicion; immediate isolation prevents exposure of susceptible pregnant women (highest risk of congenital rubella syndrome at <20 weeks) and the public health unit coordinates contact tracing and serology (CDNA Series of National Guidelines: Rubella; NCIRS: Rubella).
source_refs: CDNA Series of National Guidelines: Rubella

options.text: Send rubella IgM and IgG serology and discharge home with no isolation pending the laboratory result before considering notification to the public health unit
options.rationale: Notification is on clinical suspicion in Australia and cannot await laboratory confirmation; failure to isolate exposes susceptible pregnant contacts (CDNA: Rubella).
source_refs: CDNA Series of National Guidelines: Rubella

options.text: Administer MMR vaccination immediately to the patient and to any pregnant contacts in the clinic as urgent post-exposure prophylaxis for rubella
options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in pregnancy; vaccinating the index case after symptom onset does not modify the current illness, although catch-up vaccination should be planned for the patient when convalescent (NCIRS: Rubella; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Give intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg empirically pending blood cultures and PCR for meningococcal disease and admit for 24 hours of cardiovascular monitoring
options.rationale: Meningococcal disease produces a non-blanching purpuric rash with systemic toxicity, not a fine blanching face-down maculopapular rash with post-auricular nodes (eTG: Meningococcal disease).

options.text: Treat as measles with high-dose oral vitamin A and MMR post-exposure prophylaxis for unvaccinated household contacts within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: Measles requires Koplik spots, cough, coryza and conjunctivitis with a face-down rash that is BRICK-RED and confluent; this presentation lacks the Koplik spots, prodrome and rash intensity, and the lymphadenopathy pattern fits rubella (NCIRS: Measles).

explanation.summary: Suspected rubella in an unvaccinated patient requires immediate isolation and clinical notification because of the catastrophic risk to non-immune pregnant contacts (congenital rubella syndrome); CDNA mandates notification on suspicion (CDNA: Rubella).
key_points: Face-to-caudal rash + post-auricular/sub-occipital nodes + mild arthralgia is the classical rubella triad (NCIRS).
Highest CRS risk if maternal infection at <16 weeks gestation (NCIRS).
MMR contraindicated in pregnancy - vaccinate post-partum (Australian Immunisation Handbook).
explanation.pearls: Australia has eliminated endemic rubella - clusters now driven by under-vaccinated migrant or returning-traveller cases.

sources.label: CDNA Series of National Guidelines: Rubella

subtopic_detail: Rubella in an unvaccinated migrant teenager and public-health priorities

stem: A 12-year-old boy with known peanut allergy and mild persistent asthma is brought to a metropolitan paediatric ED after a school excursion exposure. Witnesses describe sudden generalised urticaria, lip swelling, vomiting, audible wheeze and progression to a brief loss of postural tone within 15 minutes of eating a granola bar. The school nurse administered intramuscular adrenaline 300 microgram via an EpiPen at 10 minutes and a second dose was given by paramedics 8 minutes later with sustained improvement. On arrival to ED 45 minutes after the initial reaction he is alert, with HR 96, BP 108/64, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 2 L oxygen, with no audible wheeze, no stridor, mild residual peri-oral oedema and clearing urticaria. He has had no further drop in BP, no recurrence of wheeze, and no further vomiting. The family asks whether he can be discharged after a short observation. His usual asthma is well-controlled with no recent exacerbations.

data_table.Pre-hospital: 2 doses IM adrenaline (school then paramedics, 8 min apart) with sustained improvement
data_table.Vital signs on arrival: HR 96, BP 108/64, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 2 L O2
data_table.Examination: Alert; mild residual peri-oral oedema; no wheeze, no stridor; clearing urticaria
data_table.Background: Known peanut allergy; mild persistent asthma on ICS; well-controlled

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate disposition and observation plan?

options.text: Discharge home now with oral cetirizine 10 mg daily, a written ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions, a single in-date EpiPen and follow-up review in primary care within 48 hours of discharge
options.rationale: Early discharge after severe anaphylaxis - especially with asthma comorbidity and a requirement for repeat adrenaline - underestimates the risk of biphasic reaction; ASCIA recommends prolonged observation in this scenario (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Observe in the ED short-stay unit for exactly 4 hours from the last adrenaline dose and discharge if asymptomatic with the standard discharge bundle and no extended observation despite severity features
options.rationale: Four hours is the minimum standard observation after uncomplicated anaphylaxis; severe features (repeat adrenaline, asthma, transient loss of postural tone) justify longer observation under ASCIA guidance (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Admit for at least 6-12 hours of monitored observation given severe features (two adrenaline doses, asthma, transient collapse) and discharge with updated Anaphylaxis Action Plan, two in-date adrenaline autoinjectors, ICS optimisation and immunology referral
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends extended observation (commonly at least 6-12 hours) after severe anaphylaxis or where any risk factor for biphasic or protracted reaction is present (need for repeat adrenaline, asthma comorbidity, hypotension/collapse, severe initial reaction); discharge should include an updated action plan, two adrenaline autoinjectors, asthma optimisation and specialist follow-up (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis; ASCIA Action Plans).

options.text: Admit to PICU for 24 hours of intubated airway monitoring with a continuous intravenous adrenaline infusion titrated to MAP and prophylactic salbutamol nebuliser regardless of current clinical stability
options.rationale: He is currently stable without airway compromise; intubation and adrenaline infusion are reserved for refractory anaphylaxis with airway/circulatory failure (ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7; ASCIA).

options.text: Discharge after 1 hour of observation with a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as routine pharmacological prevention of a biphasic reaction without extended ED observation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions and one-hour observation is inadequate; ASCIA emphasises observation duration over routine steroid use (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

explanation.summary: Severe anaphylaxis (need for repeat adrenaline, asthma comorbidity, transient collapse) warrants extended monitored observation - commonly 6-12 hours - with discharge planning that includes an updated Anaphylaxis Action Plan, two adrenaline autoinjectors and immunology referral (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).
key_points: Risk factors for protracted/biphasic reaction extend observation beyond 4 hours (ASCIA).
Steroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions (ASCIA).
Discharge bundle: action plan, two devices, trigger education, asthma optimisation, specialist referral (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: PBS allows two adrenaline autoinjectors per prescription - always prescribe two so one stays at school.

subtopic_detail: Anaphylaxis with biphasic risk - disposition decision

stem: A 10-year-old girl presents to ED with a third episode in six months of recurrent, non-itchy, non-erythematous swelling of the face, hands and tongue lasting 36-72 hours each time, often preceded by colicky abdominal pain. There is never any urticaria, no hypotension, no respiratory distress and no clear food, medication or insect trigger. Two previous ED visits resulted in administration of intramuscular adrenaline, intravenous hydrocortisone and chlorphenamine with no apparent response and slow spontaneous resolution. Her father has had similar episodes since childhood and her paternal uncle died from a 'throat swelling' aged 28. On examination today she is comfortable with non-pitting, non-erythematous swelling of the lips and left hand, no airway compromise, soft non-tender abdomen and normal vital signs. She has no known food, drug or environmental allergy and no asthma..

data_table.Examination: Non-pitting, non-erythematous lip and left hand swelling; no airway compromise; no urticaria; soft abdomen
data_table.Family history: Father with recurrent episodes since childhood; paternal uncle died from throat swelling age 28

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation and acute management?

options.text: Send serum tryptase during the current attack and commence intravenous adrenaline infusion with hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as for refractory anaphylaxis unresponsive to intramuscular adrenaline
options.rationale: Tryptase rises in mast-cell mediated reactions, not in bradykinin-driven HAE; mast-cell-targeted therapy (adrenaline, steroids, antihistamines) is ineffective in HAE attacks (ASCIA: Angioedema; UK HAEi/Australasian HAE consensus).

options.text: Send C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function (and consider SERPING1 testing); treat acute attack with C1 inhibitor concentrate (or icatibant) and arrange immunology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis planning
options.rationale: Low C4 between attacks plus dominantly inherited recurrent non-urticarial angioedema unresponsive to anti-allergic therapy is classical for HAE; diagnosis is confirmed by C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function, with SERPING1 testing in select cases, and acute attacks are treated with C1 inhibitor concentrate or icatibant (a bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema; Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy HAE Position).

options.text: Send total IgE and a paediatric food-specific IgE panel and commence daily non-sedating antihistamine prophylaxis with up-titration as needed for symptom control
options.rationale: IgE testing is irrelevant - HAE is bradykinin-mediated and antihistamines do not prevent attacks; this is a common diagnostic delay scenario (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

options.text: Refer for contrast-enhanced abdominal CT and outpatient gastroenterology workup for the recurrent colicky abdominal pain in isolation without further immunology investigation
options.rationale: Bowel-wall oedema from HAE causes the colicky abdominal pain; isolated GI workup misses the unifying systemic diagnosis (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

options.text: Commence daily oral tranexamic acid as immediate definitive long-term prophylaxis without C1 inhibitor testing or specialist immunology input at this presentation
options.rationale: Long-term prophylaxis decisions in paediatric HAE require confirmed diagnosis, attack frequency assessment and specialist input; tranexamic acid is sometimes used in children but is not first-line empirical therapy and does not address the acute attack (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

explanation.summary: Recurrent non-urticarial angioedema lasting days, with abdominal pain, unresponsive to adrenaline/steroids/antihistamines, with a positive family history and low C4 is HAE; confirm with C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function and treat acute attacks with C1 inhibitor concentrate or icatibant (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).
key_points: Low C4 between attacks is a useful screening clue (ASCIA).
Bradykinin-mediated - adrenaline/antihistamines/steroids ineffective (ASCIA).
Long-term prophylaxis options: C1-INH replacement, attenuated androgens (rarely in children), tranexamic acid, lanadelumab (specialist) (ASCIA).
explanation.pearls: Family history of fatal 'throat swelling' is a near-pathognomonic red flag for HAE - always ask about it in recurrent angioedema.

subtopic_detail: Hereditary angioedema versus allergic angioedema in a paediatric patient

stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED with a 4-day history of progressive fever, malaise, facial swelling and a widespread morbilliform rash that started on the trunk and spread to the limbs and face. He was started on carbamazepine 5 weeks ago for newly diagnosed focal epilepsy. There is no preceding URTI, no new foods, no other new medications and no insect exposure. On arrival temperature is 38.9 deg C, HR 122, RR 24, SpO2 97% RA, BP 110/65. Examination shows facial oedema, a confluent erythematous morbilliform eruption covering 70 percent of the body surface area without Nikolsky sign or epidermal sloughing, no mucositis, and palpable cervical, axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Investigations: WCC 18.0 x10^9/L with 12% eosinophils, ALT 320 U/L, ALP 250 U/L, creatinine 95 micromol/L (baseline 55), CRP 90 mg/L. Blood film shows atypical lymphocytes.

data_table.Examination: Facial oedema; confluent morbilliform rash 70% BSA; no Nikolsky, no mucositis; generalised lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.0 (12% eosinophils), ALT 320, ALP 250, creatinine 95 (baseline 55), CRP 90; atypical lymphocytes on film

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Continue carbamazepine at the current dose, treat as a self-limiting viral exanthem with supportive paracetamol and oral cetirizine, and review in clinic in 7 days
options.rationale: The temporal relationship to a known DRESS-trigger anticonvulsant with eosinophilia, hepatitis, lymphadenopathy and facial oedema is diagnostic of DRESS; continuing the drug worsens organ injury (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ASCIA: Drug allergy).

options.text: Stop carbamazepine immediately, admit for organ-function monitoring, commence systemic corticosteroid (e.g. prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) and consult dermatology and immunology; document the reaction and avoid aromatic anticonvulsants
options.rationale: DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) typically presents 2-8 weeks after starting culprit drugs (aromatic anticonvulsants, sulfonamides, allopurinol, vancomycin); definitive management is immediate withdrawal of the culprit, admission for organ surveillance, systemic corticosteroid for moderate-severe disease and specialist input; cross-reactivity among aromatic anticonvulsants is well documented (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ASCIA: Drug allergy in children; DermNet NZ: DRESS).

options.text: Diagnose Stevens-Johnson syndrome based on fever and rash and arrange transfer to a tertiary burns unit for crystalloid fluid resuscitation and burns-style dressing care
options.rationale: SJS/TEN is characterised by epidermal sloughing, Nikolsky sign and mucositis - none present here; DRESS is the correct SCAR phenotype in this case (DermNet NZ: SJS).

options.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin 80 mg/kg/day and obtain urgent echocardiography to exclude coronary artery aneurysms
options.rationale: Kawasaki requires fever >=5 days plus specific criteria (conjunctivitis, mucosal change, peripheral changes, polymorphous rash, lymphadenopathy) and would not explain eosinophilia and a 5-week post-drug temporal link (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

options.text: Switch carbamazepine to phenytoin at an equivalent maintenance dose and monitor liver function tests weekly without specific dermatology or immunology referral
options.rationale: Aromatic anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, phenobarbitone) cross-react in DRESS and SJS/TEN; switching within the class risks recurrence (ASCIA: Drug allergy in children).

explanation.summary: DRESS is a delayed (2-8 week) systemic drug hypersensitivity with fever, morbilliform rash, facial oedema, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia and organ involvement (commonly hepatitis); management is immediate culprit withdrawal, organ surveillance, systemic corticosteroid for moderate-severe disease, and avoidance of aromatic-anticonvulsant cross-reactors (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions).
key_points: Triggers: aromatic anticonvulsants, sulfonamides, allopurinol, vancomycin (ASCIA).
RegiSCAR criteria help score probability (DermNet NZ: DRESS).
Mortality up to 10% from hepatitis or reactivation of HHV-6 (DermNet NZ).
explanation.pearls: HLA-B*15:02 carriage (more common in some Asian ancestries) raises carbamazepine SJS/TEN risk - screen where indicated by ethnicity per Australian Medicines Handbook.

subtopic_detail: DRESS syndrome recognition after anticonvulsant exposure

stem: A 15-year-old girl with a 14-week history of daily widespread itchy wheals appearing without identifiable trigger and lasting less than 24 hours per individual lesion, with occasional lip swelling, is reviewed in a paediatric allergy clinic. She has been taking cetirizine 10 mg daily for 8 weeks with partial benefit but daily lesions continue and sleep is broken. She has no anaphylaxis, no respiratory or cardiovascular features, no joint pain, no fever and no systemic upset. Investigations show normal FBC, normal CRP, normal ESR, normal LFTs, normal TSH and negative coeliac serology. There is no preceding infection, no new medication, no food trigger and no physical trigger (heat, cold, pressure, exercise). Symptoms are interfering with her schoolwork and mood.

data_table.History: 14 weeks daily wheals <24 hours each; occasional lip swelling; no anaphylaxis or systemic features
data_table.Trial: Cetirizine 10 mg daily for 8 weeks - partial response only
data_table.Investigations: FBC, CRP, ESR, LFTs, TSH, coeliac serology all normal

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue cetirizine 10 mg daily at the standard dose and add a 6-week course of oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg daily with no taper, then reassess for biologic therapy
options.rationale: Long-term oral corticosteroids are not recommended in chronic spontaneous urticaria because of adverse effects with no sustained benefit and high relapse on withdrawal (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria; eTG: Urticaria).

options.text: Up-titrate the non-sedating H1 antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose (e.g. cetirizine 40 mg/day or equivalent) and review at 4 weeks before considering omalizumab
options.rationale: ASCIA and international urticaria guidance recommend stepwise up-dosing of a non-sedating H1 antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose for chronic spontaneous urticaria failing standard-dose therapy, with omalizumab as third-line for refractory disease (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria; EAACI/GA2LEN/WAO guideline endorsed by ASCIA).

options.text: Add a sedating antihistamine such as promethazine 0.25 mg/kg at bedtime as the primary add-on therapy for chronic urticaria refractory to standard-dose cetirizine
options.rationale: Sedating antihistamines provide symptomatic relief at night but are not recommended as primary add-on in chronic urticaria due to cognitive and developmental concerns in children; up-titration of the non-sedating agent is preferred (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).

options.text: Start intramuscular adrenaline 0.3 mg twice weekly as suppressive therapy until the wheals subside, with a written ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan for school staff
options.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for acute anaphylaxis; there is no role for prophylactic adrenaline in chronic urticaria (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

options.text: Commence ciclosporin 3 mg/kg/day in divided doses as the preferred next-line agent before any consideration of antihistamine up-titration or biologic omalizumab therapy
options.rationale: Ciclosporin is a fourth-line option after both antihistamine up-titration and omalizumab have failed; it is not before antihistamine up-titration (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).

explanation.summary: Chronic spontaneous urticaria failing standard-dose non-sedating H1 antihistamine is managed by stepwise up-titration of the non-sedating antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose; omalizumab is reserved for those who do not respond (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).
key_points: Avoid systemic corticosteroids for chronic urticaria - relapse on withdrawal (ASCIA).
Investigations beyond FBC/CRP/TFT only if specific clinical suspicion (ASCIA).
Omalizumab is PBS-listed in Australia for chronic spontaneous urticaria refractory to up-titrated H1 antihistamine.
explanation.pearls: Urticaria with INDIVIDUAL lesions lasting >24 hours, purpura or systemic features should prompt evaluation for urticarial vasculitis.

subtopic_detail: Chronic spontaneous urticaria - second-line management

stem: A previously well, immunocompetent 4-year-old boy is brought to ED on day 4 of varicella. The parents report new escalating severe pain in the left thigh disproportionate to skin appearance, a fresh fever spike to 39.6 deg C after defervescence yesterday, and reduced weight-bearing. He has not been on ibuprofen. On examination he is unwell-appearing, HR 160, RR 36, SpO2 97% RA, BP 88/50, capillary refill 3 seconds and cool peripheries. Over the left antero-medial thigh there is a 6 cm area of dusky violaceous skin around two coalesced varicella lesions, exquisite tenderness extending beyond the visible edge, indurated subcutaneous tissue and no crepitus. There are typical varicella lesions at multiple stages elsewhere. WCC 22 x10^9/L with left shift, CRP 280 mg/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 80 micromol/L (baseline 45), Na 132 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6, HR 160, RR 36, SpO2 97% RA, BP 88/50, CRT 3 s
data_table.Examination: 6 cm dusky violaceous patch around varicella lesions on left thigh; tenderness beyond visible edge; indurated subcutaneous tissue; no crepitus
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 (left shift), CRP 280, lactate 4.2, creatinine 80 (baseline 45), Na 132

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Continue oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg six-hourly, observe in the short-stay unit for 4 hours and discharge home if the pain settles with simple analgesia and reassurance
options.rationale: This is invasive bacterial soft tissue infection complicating varicella with septic shock physiology - delay risks death; discharge is unsafe (RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis; eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Resuscitate with crystalloid, administer empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Group A streptococcus and Staphylococcus aureus (including clindamycin for toxin suppression) and arrange urgent surgical assessment for source control
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, dusky skin, induration beyond the visible margin, fresh fever after defervescence on day 4 of varicella and septic shock physiology indicate invasive Group A streptococcal soft-tissue infection (potentially necrotising fasciitis); resuscitation, empirical broad cover including clindamycin for toxin suppression and urgent surgical exploration are mandatory (RCH CPG: Cellulitis; eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection; RCH CPG: Sepsis).

options.text: Apply topical mupirocin 2% ointment three times daily to the dusky area and commence oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for presumed impetiginised varicella lesions
options.rationale: Topical and oral therapy are inadequate for invasive soft-tissue infection with septic shock physiology (eTG: Skin and soft tissue infections).

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as definitive monotherapy for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome without empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics or surgical input
options.rationale: IVIG is an adjunct used in streptococcal toxic shock syndrome and necrotising fasciitis but does not replace early empirical antibiotics and surgical source control (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

options.text: Obtain urgent MRI of the left thigh with contrast to characterise fascial involvement before commencing intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical consultation
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis - imaging must not delay resuscitation, antibiotics and surgical exploration (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

explanation.summary: Worsening pain disproportionate to skin findings, fresh fever after defervescence, induration beyond visible margins and septic shock on day 4 of varicella signal invasive Group A streptococcal soft-tissue infection (possible necrotising fasciitis); management is resuscitation, empirical broad antibiotics including clindamycin for toxin suppression, and urgent surgical exploration (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).
key_points: Varicella is a strong risk factor for invasive GAS soft-tissue infection (RCH CPG).
Clindamycin added for exotoxin suppression (eTG).
Imaging must not delay source control (eTG).
explanation.pearls: NSAID exposure has been epidemiologically associated with increased severity of GAS soft-tissue infection in varicella - paracetamol is preferred for varicella fever in Australian paediatric practice.

subtopic_detail: Varicella complication in an immunocompetent child requiring escalation

stem: An 8-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED on day 3 of a febrile illness with progressive painful rash. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 9 days ago for recurrent UTI. Over the past 24 hours he has developed conjunctival redness with photophobia, painful oral erosions, dysphagia and dusky atypical targetoid macules on the trunk and face that are becoming confluent. On examination temperature is 39.0 deg C, HR 140, RR 30, SpO2 96% RA, BP 95/55. There are haemorrhagic crusted lips, fibrinous oral mucosal erosions, conjunctival injection with serous discharge, urethral meatal erosion, and dusky atypical targetoid lesions over approximately 18 percent of body surface area with Nikolsky sign positive in two areas. Investigations show WCC 12 x10^9/L, CRP 70 mg/L, ALT 85 U/L, creatinine 90 micromol/L (baseline 50), Na 134 mmol/L, glucose 5.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L. He has no immune deficiency and is otherwise well-grown. The retrieval team and burns unit are en route.
